explanation_md: On virtually all mainstream platforms `sizeof(int)` is 4, so the ternary selects `"32-bit int"`. This is a simple size check with a deterministic result on common hardware; no UB is involved.

prompt_md: Why is `using namespace std;` considered harmful inside header files?

options.a: It prevents the standard library from being used at all
options.b: It makes compile times significantly longer for every file
options.c: It pollutes the namespace of every translation unit that includes the header, risking name collisions
options.d: It only works in .cpp files, causing a compile error in headers

explanation_md: A `using namespace std;` directive in a header is injected into every file that includes it. This can cause ambiguous symbol errors or silently call the wrong overload when user-defined names clash with std names. Using `std::` prefix explicitly avoids this.

prompt_md: Which of the following best describes the purpose of `#pragma once` at the top of a header file?

options.a: It tells the compiler to optimize the file for speed
options.b: It prevents the header's contents from being processed more than once per translation unit
options.c: It marks the header as part of the C++ standard library
options.d: It forces the file to be included before any other header

explanation_md: `#pragma once` is an include guard: the compiler skips subsequent inclusions of the same file. Without it, a header included transitively through multiple paths would be processed multiple times, causing duplicate definition errors.

options.a: `std::endl` flushes the buffer on every iteration, causing excessive I/O overhead
options.b: The loop variable `i` should be `std::size_t`, not `int`
options.c: `std::cout` is not thread-safe and corrupts output in loops
options.d: The string literal `"Hello"` is re-evaluated on each iteration

explanation_md: `std::endl` outputs `'\n'` AND flushes the underlying buffer. Flushing 100,000 times is expensive. Replacing `std::endl` with `'\n'` allows the output to be buffered and written in large chunks, which is dramatically faster.

prompt_md: In modern C++, which approach is preferred for managing heap-allocated objects?

options.a: Always use raw `new` and `delete` for maximum control
options.b: Use `malloc` and `free` for compatibility with C
options.c: Use `std::unique_ptr` or `std::shared_ptr` so ownership and cleanup are automatic
options.d: Avoid heap allocation entirely and only use stack variables

explanation_md: Smart pointers tie object lifetime to scope via RAII: the destructor calls `delete` automatically, even if an exception is thrown. Raw `new`/`delete` requires every exit path to explicitly free, which is error-prone. Stack-only allocation isn't always feasible for large or long-lived objects.
